rows.title: Shadow Day
rows.publicNote:
Met a nice lady, whose grandson had been murdered - collateral target. He was killed by a 7.62 mm round. It was sad. He was only 11-years old. I cried. She told me she moved his memorial to her yard to care for him because other kids kept messing with it where it had been, previously.

It was a very nice day. My first experience walking a route. It didn’t seem like I had walked anywhere near 10 miles. I was surprised to check the app, later, to see that I had walked 11.4 miles.

I’m feeling pretty good! I get a sense that this can be a peaceful job - weather permitting.

rows.title: Day 1 - OJI
rows.publicNote:
This was a “hilly” route. Known as “Twykenham Hills”. I noticed, one thing to be careful of is watching where I’m walking - I almost rolled my ankle twice.

The second half of the route was mounted.

rows.title: Day 3 - OJI
rows.publicNote: It rained. I’m not sure if it rained a lot, but I got soaked. My truck windshield leaked right on my foot. I couldn’t see a damn thing because of my glasses. I didn’t have a towel with me. I used the extra shirt I carry with me to dry off. Luckily, I did pack a lens cloth and was able to clean my glasses and continue.

rows.title: Today Was Hot
rows.publicNote:
I went on my own today. I did the “aux routes”, which means small routes too big to be their own routes. I got it done though - had no idea what I was doing or where best to park for the loops. I pulled down my casing wrong, or there were just too big of gaps without flats. I referenced the flats and the DPS to figure out where each leg should be. I had a hard time finding the right point of reference because there were big gaps in addresses along the same road - businesses either not on the route, or not with any mail for that day. I felt like I had read the leg wrong, or that I should have just parked in the middle and run 3 or 4 legs from the same point. It’s a narrow, but long, block and I ended up doing both sides. The problem was, there were some businesses on each street with big gaps between them. I kept pulling out my phone and using GPS when I thought it might be better to just go back and move the truck up. And before anyone says “why didn’t I check the route book”? Well, I looked for it and didn’t find one. I’ll ask, but I don’t think our aux routes has a route book. It’s probably just up to me to figure out how best to balance it, but I’ll ask.

It rained this morning and we had to wait on DPS mail to arrive. I finished my aux routes and got sent back out to deliver packages.
rows.notes: No journal HI; Journal warmer than archive; Precip 1.1" (rain)

rows.publicNote:
Today was hot. But I was prepared for it… sort of. I really need to get some sweat rags or cooling towels to wipe my face with. I was getting a lot of sweat in my eyes. I confirmed there is not a route book for AUX routes. I asked another carrier how they would run the route and they suggested, basically what I was thinking - to park in the middle and do all of the legs on the two sides of the block. It was a lot of walking, and I still think I could have done it better. I had to walk up one side like it was dead (no mail), even though it wasn’t, because that’s how it’s sorted. After I finished the AUX routes, I went back out for two more relays on another route. Today was my longest distance walked, yet.

I received a high form of union shop compliment today! After calling myself a dumbass someone said they’d give me a clipboard if I kept at it. Then, one of my coworkers who I trained with said I was WAY too smart for that.

I’m quickly getting acclimated to the heat, but today was very hot - a 103 degree heat index. Luckily, I had a cooling towel to wipe sweat away. By the end of my walking, at about 1 PM, I only had a couple more relays to do, and I was beat tired. I would drive to the next relay and sit for a few minutes before getting out. I also noticed, I was walking very slow. When I walked in the door back at the station, one of my coworkers could tell I looked exhausted.

But I survived! Ibuprofen and “I see the minnow fin” for recovery. Tomorrow will be another hot one!

I got “attacked” by a tiny puppy today! It was cute. I didn’t spray it, obviously.

I took a different route today. We were missing coverage for several routes and I got shifted to split a route with someone. I took the bulk of it. There were a lot of flats - without having experienced this route before, I’m not sure, but I’d guess we had more than usual.

Unfortunately, I was about 45 minutes from finishing it all. I had two more small relays left to do.

I met a nice lady who gave me a frozen water. Overall, it was a good day. I just wish I would have finished, but it was my first time touching this route, I’ll finish it next time, likely.

I got to do some mounted delivery today with a bunch of packages. They were all small, so I was able to organize them in bins by delivery sequence. I had a weird issue locating an address because the circular part of a ‘6’  was missing, and looked like a ‘1’.
I forgot my hat in the car… twice. I finished the aux routes and then took a piece of another route. Being Saturday, there were more people around in the neighborhoods.

Well, I almost got bit by a dog today  - two separate encounters, actually. The first house was one I’ve been delivering to and hadn’t even expected they had a dog. It turns out the owner was outside with their two dogs. They ran at me and I yelled “Shit, Fuck!” as I fumbled …and failed to grab my spray. Luckily, yelling at the dogs scared them enough to stop them. I laughed and apologized, said some gibberish that didn’t make any sense as I walked away.

Then, another encounter, just a few houses down! I was ready, actually. I knew they had a dog, and I saw the front door was open. I went up the porch, two dogs came running, jumped on the storm door, it opened and I ninja kicked it shut, put the mail in the box and left after I knew the owner had control of their dogs. I will be writing a card for that house with preventative measure and warnings for other carriers.

Otherwise, today was a really nice day. It was an ideal weather day. I had tons of energy and even felt like I could have a “second wind”.

I got kind of lucky today. We were short routes, and the way they got split allowed me to do mostly “dismounts” and some mounted delivery, today. I only did two relays in the rain and they were very light. It was a pretty light day - I’ll take it, given it rained most of the day. My feet are pretty wet, but not bad.

Today was great! I took a whole route. I didn’t finish, but I will next time. I brought back about 30 - 40 minutes of mounted delivery. I only brought it back because I’m still onboarding and they have a new program that won’t allow new PTFs to work more than 8 hours for the first four weeks.

When it’s the first time touching a route it takes extra time to get to know where everything is. I’ll finish it the next time I get a chance.

rows.title: I’m Starting to Feel Pretty Good!
rows.publicNote:
Okay, I put some weight back on, but I’m feeling really good. I have tons of energy and I hurt less.

Well… I did go to physical therapy on my day off. I’m finally getting tired of my hip and back being sore. My hip and back have been really stiff and sore after a shift. It turns out I have weak transverse-abdominal muscles, and despite my left side hurting my right side is weaker than my left. I’ve been doing the stretches and trying to do the weird belly button crunch thing they showed me, and today I didn’t have any soreness or stiffness in my back.

This route is dense. The number of houses, spacing, and the fact that each street gets longer as you go. The first park point covers a short street, 1600 to 1800, and a small no outlet 1900. At each subsequent park point they houses went farther up a hill until it reached 2200, eventually.

Also, this particular route has really bad sidewalks and uneven ground to traverse. 

I did not take a Gatorade with me today, that may be why I felt tired by the end of the shift. Maybe I didn’t eat enough. I only ate four beef sticks.

I felt pretty good today. But to be fair, I didn’t walk that much. At least, it didn’t feel like it; and it was, relative to other days.

The route I was on today was mostly “dismount” or “in & out” deliveries. That’s where the houses are too far apart to be a simple walking route - but even that depends. Performing a dismount delivery, one drives up to each house, parks the truck, curbs the wheel, dismounts, closes and locks the door, makes the delivery (some houses are up really big hills), gets back in the truck, drive and park at the next house, rinse and repeat.

Realistically, that’s as tedious as it sounds - it’s not that hard, as you get used to doing the repeated motions - but I look at the mail, look at the houses and try to park by the second sequential house and do both at the same time, or if there are four or five houses in a cul-de-sac.
The heat drained me today.

Today was awful because of the heat. I also delivered on a route I haven't done before, and for the first time I was actually confused, at least more than briefly. 

There were these streets that ran sharp diagonals and one street that kept showing up at the beginning and end of each run. Unfortunately, the picture I took of all the park points written out, I wasn't able to follow it very well - it had me thinking the one confusing street was one relay at one point.

I know better now, and understand that same street as an anchoring point of the entire route, now. However, there were still a lot some houses I couldn't find.

Today I got paced by management in extreme heat.

I was on unfamiliar territory again, in the middle of one of the hottest stretches of the year, already trying to learn the route, manage the mail, stay safe, and make it back by the time I was told to return. Then management came out and watched me work because they’re concerned about my speed.

Of course I moved faster while they were there. That is what pressure does. But moving faster in dangerous heat does not mean the pace is safe. It just means I was being pushed.

I fell. I had to stop and catch my breath. I had to reset because my body was telling me very clearly that the pace was not sustainable in that heat.

I am new, but I am not stupid. I know the difference between being coached and being pressured. I know the difference between learning the job and being pushed past a safe limit. I want to succeed here, and I am willing to work hard, but I will not be pushed into hurting myself just to satisfy someone else’s numbers.

The mail matters. The job matters. But I am not going to kill myself for a route time.

The heat has killed me. I’m struggling to finish routes. To be fair, it has been triple digit heat index the last four days and management keeps putting me on unfamiliar routes. However, as of today I’ll be assigned to a route for a little while.
I’m not going to lie. The reason the USPS has such high turnover is because of poor management.

Today was hot. The DPS was out of order, second day in a row.

I’m assigned the same route all week but only after two days on it,  I’m struggling to get it done. To be fair, yesterday, I had to case it myself. That takes time when it’s the first time putting eyes on a route. The DPS count was over 1,200, and it’s my opinion that people on this route order a lot of parcels. When all said and done, one will walk 12-13 miles on this route.

When I didn’t finish the route yesterday, the DPS was left for me to carry today. They had me come in at 09:00 and said someone would case, pull down, and flag it for me. They had another new guy that had never cased that route casing for me. I had to pull it down myself, it didn’t get flagged. I was still slow to get out for the day, as a result.  Then, they had me start the route from the point where I left off, the day prior. Once I got to the points I had carried yesterday, I felt more confident, could split the mail and grab it much faster, didn’t need GPS to know where I was going. However, I still struggled to finish, today. The heat compounded and slowed me down towards the end. I drove a pro master truck, which is supposed to have A/C, but mine just blew warm air. The pro masters get HOT! I drank a lot, and when I finally got the chance to go to the bathroom the color and volume check wasn’t ideal.

I was tired to say the least.

I finished the hard route today! It’s a BIG route. The swing carrier said it was a big route,  and today I spoke with a customer, who said one of the other carriers also said it was a big route. But still, I finished it. I made very negligible mistakes, because I knew the route. Only twice I forgot to drop a package, but I knew when I would be on the opposite side of the street, again and dropped them, then. I only needed my GPS once. I’ve learned to used use the package lookahead feature, and live by it, and once I know a route, it’s not just numbers on a screen.  


I should say, I didn’t walk everything. I ran it modified as “in-&-outs”. It made sense, or else I would have skipped a lot of houses on each block, as there was no mail for them, anyways. I flagged all the DPS myself and got out earlier than I had the other two days. It still took me till about 6:30 PM to finish it all. I don’t think I could walk 11-12 miles (about how long I estimate this route would require, and finish it. I think this route requires knowing the house locations (literally where they are) and active splitting of the route as you go - running 3-4 houses from a specific park point before moving down the block, skipping several houses. That’s simply route acumen, as I’ll be referring to from now on. It’s not something a PTF should be expected to know first time, or even second time touching a route, maybe. But, I think I kicked its ass today.

Today the smoke from the wildfires in Canada was really bad, that and the heat. I sweat and drank a lot, which made it tolerable, but the smoke only got worse as the day went on. Apparently, the Great Lakes region has some of the worst air quality, resulting from the fires. I hate to say it, but I should have worn a mask today.
I crushed the big route today, plus ran parcels. I even walked at a nice, relaxed pace. I just knew the route and how to run it - route acumen.

Today sucked. I had the hard route with two types of advertisements. I couldn’t run the route as the modified in-&-outs. I had to walk ALL of it. No houses were skipped.

I called to let them know. They sent help, I cut half of what was left, he and I ran about an hour and finished it all out.
